category_signature.one_sentence_summary: This category behaves like an architect-first AI platform leadership market: show enterprise architecture, production delivery, multi-cloud reliability, governance, and stakeholder translation, while avoiding inflated executive or org-building claims unless explicitly evidenced.
distinctive_signals: Architect-heavy title mix: Solutions Architect 20/35 (57.1%) and AI Architect 7/35 (20.0%) outweigh classic executive leadership labels; platform design appears in 35/35 (100.0%).
Role scope is mostly senior IC or player-coach, not large-org management: IC 22/35 (62.9%), player-coach 8/35 (22.9%), hiring 5/35 (14.3%), org-building 5/35 (14.3%), budget/P&L 0/35 (0.0%).

Differentiation comes from production AI architecture, governance, and reliability rather than advanced experimentation: agents/orchestration 9/35 (25.7%), guardrails/governance 8/35 (22.9%), RAG 4/35 (11.4%), advanced experimentation-heavy 0/35 (0.0%), advanced academic requirement 0/35 (0.0%).
citations: Title families: Solutions Architect 20/35 (57.1%), AI Architect 7/35 (20.0%), Engineering Manager 3/35 (8.6%).
Role scope: IC 22/35 (62.9%), player-coach 8/35 (22.9%), director 3/35 (8.6%), manager 2/35 (5.7%).
Architecture: platform design 35/35 (100.0%); greenfield+optimization both 16/35 (45.7%); multi-cloud preference 15/35 (42.9%).

headline_pattern.recommended_structure: [Actual evidence-backed architect title] | Enterprise AI / Solutions Architecture | Multi-Cloud Platform Design | Governance, Reliability, Delivery
evidence_first_rules: Lead with the most credible architect-family title you have held; this aligns with Solutions Architect 20/35 (57.1%) and AI Architect 7/35 (20.0%), while management signals remain weak.
Name architecture scope explicitly in the headline: enterprise AI platforms, solutions architecture, multi-cloud, distributed systems, governance, reliability.

Only use management-heavy or executive titles if the CV proves line ownership, hiring, org-building, or budget accountability; hiring is only 5/35 (14.3%), org-building 5/35 (14.3%), budget/P&L 0/35 (0.0%).
Do not force a literal Head of AI headline when the underlying evidence is architect-led and title noise is high; keep the title candidate-safe and delivery-backed.
citations: Title families: Solutions Architect 20/35 (57.1%), AI Architect 7/35 (20.0%), Engineering Manager 3/35 (8.6%); noisy_title_mode=true.

positioning_angle: Architect-first AI platform leader who translates business and customer requirements into production-ready systems.
Player-coach delivery lead for enterprise AI and data platforms, emphasizing reliability, governance, and reusable architecture patterns over org-chart scale.
foreground: Platform design and modernization for complex systems.
Production AI delivery with governance, guardrails, and operational readiness.
Multi-cloud and distributed architecture choices tied to resilience and maintainability.
Customer-facing and cross-functional translation: communication 25/35 (71.4%), collaboration 23/35 (65.7%), stakeholder management 13/35 (37.1%).
avoid: Executive-only positioning centered on org design, large-team hiring, or budget ownership.
advanced experimentation, formal external credential, or advanced academic framing.
Single-vendor cloud branding unless delivery proof is specific to that cloud.
Regional or industry claims that appear only once or twice in the evidence.
safe_positioning: Demonstrate architecture ownership through systems designed, integrations delivered, reliability improved, and governance mechanisms implemented.
Show how you moved ambiguous AI or platform requirements into shipped capabilities, operational readiness, and reusable standards.
Position leadership as technical leadership, stakeholder translation, mentoring, and delivery influence unless formal people management is explicit.
unsafe_positioning: Claiming company-wide AI strategy, department-level hiring, or enterprise budget accountability without direct proof.
Claiming formal ownership of audits, compliance programs, or security reviews without explicit evidence.
Claiming broad executive Head of AI authority when the CV mainly shows architect or senior IC scope.
citations: Soft skills: communication 25/35 (71.4%), collaboration 23/35 (65.7%), stakeholder management 13/35 (37.1%), leadership 11/35 (31.4%), mentoring 10/35 (28.6%).
Management leadership: hiring 5/35 (14.3%), org-building 5/35 (14.3%), performance management 3/35 (8.6%), budget/P&L 0/35 (0.0%).

themes.theme: Enterprise and solutions architecture for AI/data platforms
themes.why_it_matters: This category is fundamentally architecture-led: platform design appears in 35/35 (100.0%), Solutions Architect titles in 20/35 (57.1%), and architecture is the top valued evidence type in deep analysis at 20/20 (100.0%).
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'solutions architecture', 'enterprise architecture', or 'AI platform architecture' as strongest keywords; repeat 2-3 times across headline, profile, experience, and skills if true.
themes.citation: Platform design 35/35 (100.0%); Solutions Architect 20/35 (57.1%); architecture valued evidence 20/20 (100.0%).

themes.theme: Distributed systems, integration, and scalable platform design
themes.why_it_matters: The market rewards proof that core architecture scales while staying maintainable across services and integrations, not generic AI enthusiasm.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'distributed systems', 'system integration', 'microservices', and 'APIs' as secondary keywords; mention 1-2 times each if true, and support with shipped-system examples.
themes.citation: Hard skills: microservices 5/35 (14.3%), APIs 4/35 (11.4%), scalable distributed systems 4/35 (11.4%); pain points include architecture scaling and cross-cloud microservices integration.

themes.why_it_matters: A visible pain pattern is improving complex existing application architectures, so modernization stories are more valuable than greenfield-only stories.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'modernization', 'architecture optimization', or 'platform refactor' as secondary keywords; mention 1-2 times if true and quantify what improved.
themes.citation: Greenfield split: both greenfield and optimization 16/35 (45.7%), optimization-only 5/35 (14.3%); pain point: improving complex existing application architectures 3 mentions.

themes.theme: Agentic or workflow-based AI systems in production
themes.why_it_matters: Pure model experimentation is not the center of gravity here; production AI delivery and orchestration are stronger signals than niche framework mentions.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'AI workflows', 'agent orchestration', or 'LLM platform' as secondary keywords; mention 1-2 times if true. Use framework names once only and only if true.
themes.citation: AI stack: agents/orchestration 9/35 (25.7%), RAG 4/35 (11.4%); frameworks are sparse: LangChain 2/35 (5.7%), Semantic Kernel 1/35 (2.9%).

themes.why_it_matters: This category values production readiness and safe operation more than advanced experimentation framing, making governance and evaluation evidence disproportionately useful when real.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'governance', 'guardrails', 'evaluation', and 'quality' as secondary keywords; mention 1-2 times if true and tie them to concrete controls or review mechanisms.
themes.citation: AI stack: guardrails/governance 8/35 (22.9%), evaluation/quality 3/35 (8.6%); governance is a valued evidence type in deep analysis 13/20 (65.0%).

themes.why_it_matters: Deep analysis favors delivery, reliability, and scale evidence over abstract strategy. CV bullets should show systems that shipped, ran, and stayed stable.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'delivery', 'operational readiness', 'reliability', and 'production readiness' as strongest keywords; repeat 2-3 times across profile and experience if true.
themes.citation: Deep analysis valued evidence: delivery 18/20 (90.0%), scale 17/20 (85.0%), reliability 15/20 (75.0%); success metrics include operational readiness, predictable release pipelines, resilience, and performance.

themes.why_it_matters: The role often sits at the translation layer between customer needs, sales motion, product, and engineering execution.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'stakeholder management', 'customer advisory', and 'solution design' as strongest keywords; repeat 2-3 times if true and connect them to decisions made or deals supported.
themes.citation: Soft skills: communication 25/35 (71.4%), collaboration 23/35 (65.7%), stakeholder management 13/35 (37.1%); pain point: sales leadership needs strategy support across accounts/verticals 2 mentions.

themes.why_it_matters: Leadership is present, but it is usually technical and delivery-oriented rather than managerial. This is useful when the CV can show mentoring, standards, and execution influence.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'technical leadership', 'mentoring', and 'cross-functional delivery' as secondary keywords; mention 1-2 times if true and avoid inflating to org leadership.
themes.citation: Role scope: player-coach 8/35 (22.9%), IC 22/35 (62.9%); soft skills: leadership 11/35 (31.4%), mentoring 10/35 (28.6%).

themes.theme: Reusable standards, assets, and best practices
themes.why_it_matters: This is a smaller but useful signal for candidates who have created templates, reference architectures, or shared delivery patterns.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'reference architecture', 'reusable assets', or 'best practices' as niche keywords; mention once only and only if true.
themes.citation: Pain point: broader organization needs reusable assets and shared best practices 2 mentions.

themes.why_it_matters: The cloud signal is broad rather than single-vendor. Stronger CVs show how architecture choices worked across environments, not just tool familiarity.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'multi-cloud' as a strongest keyword with 2-3 mentions if true; use specific cloud names 1-2 times each only where delivery proof exists.
themes.citation: Architecture cloud preference: multi-cloud 15/35 (42.9%), Azure 5/35 (14.3%), AWS 1/35 (2.9%); hard skills also show AWS 7/35 (20.0%), Azure 5/35 (14.3%), GCP 4/35 (11.4%).

themes.theme: Hands-on technical credibility without over-weighting one stack
themes.why_it_matters: There is no single universal technical keyword in this category; breadth matters more than centering the CV around one language or framework.
themes.ats_guidance: Use the strongest true technical keywords 2-3 times total across headline/profile/experience/skills, but only pick 4-6. Use secondary keywords 1-2 times. Use niche tools once only.
themes.citation: No hard skill reaches 60%; top hard skills are Python 10/35 (28.6%), SQL 8/35 (22.9%), AWS 7/35 (20.0%), Spark 6/35 (17.1%). Programming languages: Python 13/35 (37.1%), Java 8/35 (22.9%), SQL 8/35 (22.9%).

themes.why_it_matters: Security and compliance appear as architecture constraints, but not as broad ownership requirements. Include only if the CV proves implementation-level involvement.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'security', 'privacy', 'GDPR', or 'ISO 27001' as niche keywords; mention once only and only if true, never as a headline claim unless explicitly owned.
themes.citation: Governance/compliance top: GDPR 4/35 (11.4%), ISO 27001 1/35 (2.9%), information security 1/35 (2.9%); pain point includes security, compliance, and reliability built into architecture.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Enterprise AI platform architecture delivered to production
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Proves fit for the category's architect-first center of gravity: platform design, delivery, scale, and operational readiness.
pain_points_addressed: the broader organization needs reusable assets and shared best practices
product and engineering teams need architectural consistency
core architecture needs to scale while staying maintainable
metrics_to_include: number of workflows, plugins, or integrations
time to launch or rollout speed
adoption across teams or business units
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR or ARIS. Start with platform ambiguity or fragmentation, show the architecture you defined, then quantify shipped scope, operational readiness, and usage.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Platform design 35/35 (100.0%); deep analysis valued evidence: architecture 20/20 (100.0%), delivery 18/20 (90.0%), scale 17/20 (85.0%), reliability 15/20 (75.0%).

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Modernized a complex legacy or existing architecture into a more scalable design
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Shows the ability to improve complex existing systems, which is a repeated pain point and a strong differentiator versus greenfield-only stories.
pain_points_addressed: customers need guidance improving complex existing application architectures
core architecture needs to scale while staying maintainable
product and engineering teams need architectural consistency
metrics_to_include: reduction in failure rate or incidents
maintenance burden or developer productivity gains
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Frame the before-state clearly: complexity, reliability issues, or slow releases. Then show the architectural interventions and the measurable operational change.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Greenfield split: both greenfield and optimization 16/35 (45.7%), optimization-only 5/35 (14.3%); pain point: improving complex existing application architectures 3 mentions; success metrics include predictable release pipelines and resilience.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Designed multi-cloud or cross-platform architecture with reliability and resilience controls
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Demonstrates practical architecture judgment and operational rigor, especially valuable when paired with AI platform or API-heavy systems.
pain_points_addressed: microservices and event-driven systems need to work across cloud providers
security, compliance, and reliability must be built into the architecture
customers need help designing reliable, scalable systems
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use ARIS: state the reliability or portability risk, describe the architecture and controls, then quantify stability, recovery, latency, or cost outcomes.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Cloud preference: multi-cloud 15/35 (42.9%), Azure 5/35 (14.3%), AWS 1/35 (2.9%); deep analysis valued evidence: reliability 15/20 (75.0%), cost 6/20 (30.0%).

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Governance and safe-production mechanisms for AI systems
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Shows maturity beyond demo-stage AI by proving safe operation, quality controls, and production guardrails when the experience is real.
pain_points_addressed: security, compliance, and reliability must be built into the architecture
the broader organization needs reusable assets and shared best practices
metrics_to_include: guardrail rules or policy checks implemented
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Avoid vague 'responsible AI' language. Name the concrete mechanism used, where it sat in the architecture, and what risk or quality metric improved.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: AI stack: guardrails/governance 8/35 (22.9%), evaluation/quality 3/35 (8.6%); governance is a valued evidence type 13/20 (65.0%).

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Customer-facing architecture leadership that helped win or expand work
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Demonstrates the commercial side of the role: translating architecture into customer confidence, sales support, and delivery alignment across accounts.
pain_points_addressed: sales leadership needs support identifying strategies for growth across multiple accounts and verticals
customers need help designing reliable, scalable systems
customers need guidance improving complex existing application architectures
metrics_to_include: time from discovery to proposed architecture
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR with a clear customer problem, the architecture recommendation you shaped, and the commercial or customer outcome that followed.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Pain points: sales leadership growth support 2 mentions; reliable scalable systems on MongoDB 3 mentions; improving complex existing architectures 3 mentions; success metrics include winning deals and customer success/satisfaction.

highest_weight_roles: Solutions Architect, Enterprise Architect, or AI Architect roles with explicit platform ownership.
Technical Lead or player-coach roles that show cross-functional delivery and architectural decision-making.
Hands-on platform, backend, or data architecture roles that prove scalable systems and integration design.
expand_in_work_history: Roles where you owned architecture decisions, platform design, modernization, or complex system integration.
Roles with production AI, governance, evaluation, multi-cloud, reliability, or reusable standards evidence.
Customer-facing or stakeholder-heavy roles where you translated requirements into delivery outcomes.
compress_in_work_history: Pure people-management roles without strong architecture or delivery proof.
Generic engineering manager framing when line management scope is light or absent.
Older implementation-only work that does not connect to architecture, scale, or platform modernization.
how_to_frame_non_ai_experience: Translate non-AI architecture work into reusable signals for this category: distributed systems, event-driven design, APIs, modernization, resilience, and cross-team integration.
Frame prior backend or platform work as the operating substrate for enterprise AI delivery, not as a separate career track.
Only bridge non-AI work into cloud, governance, or industry-specific claims when the delivery evidence is explicit.
citations: Title families: Solutions Architect 20/35 (57.1%), AI Architect 7/35 (20.0%).
Deep analysis candidate mapping repeatedly values enterprise AI platform delivery, multi-provider routing, and prior distributed-systems work as fit signals.
Deep analysis specific domain years favor architecture tenure rather than pure research credentials.
Deep analysis valued evidence: architecture 20/20 (100.0%), delivery 18/20 (90.0%), scale 17/20 (85.0%), reliability 15/20 (75.0%), governance 13/20 (65.0%).

avoid_claims: Do not claim direct ownership of security reviews, audits, or compliance assessments without explicit proof.
Do not claim enterprise or government platform delivery at scale without concrete evidence.
Do not claim production Kubernetes governance if the CV only shows adjacent distributed-systems work.
Do not claim formal line management of a large engineering organization without explicit proof.
Do not claim company-wide SDLC, DORA, or architecture governance ownership without clear evidence.
Do not claim end-to-end monolith-to-microservices migration leadership if your role was partial architectural influence.
Do not claim Azure-native enterprise architecture leadership unless Azure delivery is explicitly shown.
Do not claim GDPR, PCI-DSS, ISO 42001, or ISO 27001 governance ownership without direct evidence.
Do not claim department-level hiring, performance evaluation, or budget accountability at enterprise scope without proof.
buzzword_patterns: Avoid generic 'AI strategy transformation' language without shipped-system evidence.
Avoid long lists of frameworks when framework signals are sparse and architecture/delivery signals are stronger.
Avoid 'GenAI expert' or 'AI leader' phrasing unless the experience section proves production systems, governance, and business translation.
Avoid presenting cloud names as core identity; present them as architecture decisions backed by delivery.
title_inflation_risks: Using 'Head of AI' is risky if the CV does not show formal executive scope; title evidence is noisier and more architect-led than executive-led.
Using 'Engineering Manager' is risky when people-management evidence is weak and the stronger signal is architect/player-coach.
Using 'Director' or 'VP' labels is unsafe without direct reports, hiring, org-building, or budget evidence.
research_framing_risks: Do not foreground research, publications, or academic framing; research-heavy roles are 0/35 (0.0%) and PhD requirements are 0/35 (0.0%).
Do not over-index on model training, fine-tuning, or framework jargon when orchestration, governance, delivery, and architecture are the stronger evidence-backed signals.
domain_or_region_risks: Do not imply fintech, defense, ecommerce, or omnichannel depth unless the CV explicitly proves it; domain evidence is fragmented: enterprise SaaS 6, fintech 3, defense 2, ecommerce 1.
Do not claim Arabic, Turkish, or other language-market specialization unless explicit; language evidence only shows English once and one Turkish-speaking title variant.
Do not imply UAE-specific regulatory ownership or regional transformation scope without direct evidence.
citations: Deep analysis unsafe claims list includes unsupported compliance ownership, large-scale line management, enterprise platform delivery, Azure-native leadership, and budget accountability.
Market context domain evidence is fragmented rather than concentrated.

overall_assessment.one_sentence_verdict: Good baseline fit when positioned as an architect-first AI platform leader with delivery, reliability, governance, and stakeholder translation; still a stretch for literal executive Head of AI framing.
overall_assessment.uncertainty_note: Category evidence is strong but title noise is high, and the current master-CV view is proxy-derived rather than a canonical artifact; candidate fit is materially stronger for architect/player-coach AI leadership than for formal executive scope.
citations: Blueprint: Solutions Architect 20/35 (57.1%), AI Architect 7/35 (20.0%), IC 22/35 (62.9%), player-coach 8/35 (22.9%), budget/P&L 0/35 (0.0%).

weighted_score_explanation: Coverage is strong because curated evidence already supports enterprise architecture, modernization, reliability, stakeholder translation, mentoring, and enterprise AI workflow delivery across Seven.One, Samdock, and AI projects.

Curation completeness is lower because architect-title evidence and some role-framing material remain upstream or insufficiently promoted into reusable metadata, especially the stronger architect-family title variants from the knowledge base.

Representation quality is adequate but not sharp for this category: the headline is generic, the summary is overly anchored on one modernization story, and the current project/skills emphasis overweights tooling relative to architecture, governance, and stakeholder advisory.
AI architecture fit is high because Commander-4 and Lantern prove workflow-based AI systems, evaluation, routing, guardrails, structured outputs, and operational readiness in production settings.

Leadership scope fit is moderate because player-coach leadership, mentoring, hiring participation, and stakeholder influence are supported, but formal executive authority, org-building scale, and budget ownership are not.

Impact proof is strong because the curated store contains measurable reliability, adoption, velocity, onboarding, and cost signals such as 75% incident reduction, 3 years zero downtime, 2,000 users, 42 plugins, 25% velocity improvement, and 25 production tenants.
citations: Blueprint: platform design 35/35 (100.0%), governance valued evidence 13/20 (65.0%), delivery 18/20 (90.0%), reliability 15/20 (75.0%), budget/P&L 0/35 (0.0%).

strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by Seven.One modernization, architectural runway, and DDD evidence at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:28, :54, :88.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Operational readiness, observability, and reliability leadership
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: This market values systems that ship and stay stable; the candidate has explicit production reliability proof rather than abstract strategy language.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by 75% incident reduction and 3 years zero downtime, the OpenSearch observability pipeline, MTTR-focused monitoring, and Lantern observability at :28, :71, :163, and data/master-cv/projects/lantern.md:10.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Production AI workflow platform design with governance, evaluation, and tool integration
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: This category prefers production AI architecture, guardrails, and workflow orchestration with clear operational controls.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Stakeholder translation and architectural north-star setting

strongest_supported_signals.citation: Supported by Seven.One north-star and stakeholder collaboration evidence plus earlier client delivery work at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:123, :250 and data/master-cv/roles/06_clary_icon.md:103.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Player-coach leadership through mentoring, standards, and selective hiring scope
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: The category rewards technical leadership and mentoring more than large-org management claims.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Architect-family title proof in reusable metadata
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: The category is safer when led by an architect-family title rather than a management-heavy or executive label.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Current representation leads with 'Engineering Leader / Software Architect' and role title 'Technical Lead (Addressable TV)'; stronger architect-title variants exist upstream but are not yet promoted into reusable metadata.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Architect-led scope is credible; 'Lead Software Engineer / Technical Architect' is supported upstream, but should be treated as pending curation until promoted with provenance.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Promote the architect-family title variants from the knowledge base into data/master-cv/role_metadata.json and the Seven.One role metadata so downstream CV generation can safely lead with architect framing.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Not yet curated from docs/archive/knowledge-base.md:7, :98, :268, :360; current representation remains generic at data/master-cv/role_metadata.json:3.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Stakeholder-facing architecture advisory and business translation
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: This category repeatedly rewards communication, collaboration, and stakeholder management tied to architecture decisions.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Curated evidence exists for technical vision, stakeholder collaboration, event storming, and direct client communication, but the current summary and priority view largely foreground modernization and technical stack details.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Claim technical stakeholder translation, architecture advisory, and cross-functional decision influence; avoid broader commercial or sales-ownership claims.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Surface stakeholder-facing architecture evidence earlier in summary/profile and role prioritization, especially Seven.One stakeholder collaboration and Samdock event-storming facilitation.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Underrepresented in the current representation despite curated support at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:123, :250, data/master-cv/roles/02_samdock_daypaio.md:44, and data/master-cv/roles/06_clary_icon.md:103.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Governance, guardrails, evaluation, and safe-production AI controls
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Governance and evaluation readiness are category differentiators for architect-first AI leadership roles.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Curated AI evidence includes guardrail profiles, per-silo policy enforcement, structured outputs, access control, retrieval evaluation, semantic caching, and eval-driven quality gates, but the current headline/summary do not foreground these differentiators.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Claim production AI governance mechanisms, guardrails, evaluation harnesses, and safe structured-output architecture; avoid unsupported claims of enterprise-wide compliance ownership.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Move governance/evaluation language into the summary and experience surface area ahead of framework inventories; preserve concrete mechanisms and avoid generic 'responsible AI' phrasing.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Supported by curated Commander-4 and Lantern controls at data/master-cv/projects/commander4.md:8-9, data/master-cv/projects/lantern.md:9, and data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:332, :354, but not surfaced in data/master-cv/role_metadata.json:3.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Reusable standards, reference patterns, and architecture enablement
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Reusable standards and shared patterns are useful differentiators for this market's architect/player-coach roles.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Curated evidence exists for DDD standards, ubiquitous language, architectural reviews, reusable component standards, and reusable server/template patterns, but this theme is not prominent in the current master-CV representation.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Claim engineering standards, reusable patterns, and architecture enablement where tied to concrete systems and outcomes.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Surface DDD standards, onboarding/velocity gains, and reusable pattern creation as architecture-enablement proof rather than burying them behind generic leadership phrasing.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Supported by standards and reusable-asset evidence at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:88, data/master-cv/roles/02_samdock_daypaio.md:60, and data/master-cv/roles/05_osram.md:74.

gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: The category likes multi-cloud judgment, but overclaiming here would inflate the evidence beyond what is currently proven.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Curated delivery shows AWS-heavy architecture, prior GCP experience, and multi-provider LLM endpoint routing, but not a clearly evidenced enterprise multi-cloud platform architecture mandate.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Claim AWS architecture, prior GCP exposure, and multi-provider LLM routing; do not claim broad multi-cloud platform leadership as a headline identity.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Keep cloud language precise and delivery-backed unless additional direct multi-cloud architecture evidence is curated.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Unsafe because the current evidence is AWS-led with some GCP and multi-provider routing signals, but not explicit enterprise multi-cloud ownership at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:28, data/master-cv/roles/02_samdock_daypaio.md:142, and data/master-cv/projects/lantern.md:7.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Formal Head of AI or executive org-wide AI authority

gap_analysis_top.current_state: The evidence supports technical leadership, platform lead scope, mentoring, and selective hiring involvement, but does not prove department-wide AI leadership, budget ownership, or broad org-building authority.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Position as architect-first AI platform leader or technical lead with delivery influence, not as formal executive Head of AI.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Avoid executive title inflation until direct evidence exists for line ownership, org-building, and budget accountability.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Unsafe because current evidence shows player-coach and platform-lead scope, not executive ownership, at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:182, :217, :282 and data/master-cv/role_metadata.json:3.

gap_analysis_top.signal: AI compliance or audit ownership beyond concrete implemented controls
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: The category values governance, but unsupported expansion from GDPR product work into AI or enterprise compliance ownership would create unnecessary risk.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Curated evidence supports GDPR/TCF delivery in ad-tech and guardrail/eval mechanisms in AI workflows, but not end-to-end AI compliance-program ownership.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Claim implemented compliance-sensitive architecture and AI guardrails; avoid claiming enterprise AI audit, policy, or regulatory ownership.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Keep governance wording concrete and mechanism-based unless broader ownership is curated with provenance.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Unsafe because the evidence supports GDPR/TCF execution and AI guardrails, not broad AI compliance ownership, at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:106 and data/master-cv/projects/commander4.md:8-9.

headline_safe: Technical Lead / Software Architect | Enterprise AI Workflow Platforms | Distributed Systems, Reliability, Delivery
Software Architect | Event-Driven Platform Modernization | AI Workflows, Governance, Operational Readiness
Engineering Leader / Software Architect | Enterprise Platform Architecture | Stakeholder Translation, Reliability, Delivery
profile_safe: Architect-first technical leader with 11 years of experience translating ambiguous platform and AI requirements into production systems, modernization roadmaps, and reliable delivery.

Designed and modernized distributed platforms across ad-tech and SaaS, including event-driven microservices, observability pipelines, DDD-based architecture standards, and enterprise AI workflow systems.
Built production AI capabilities around retrieval quality, structured outputs, guardrails, access control, semantic caching, and evaluation harnesses for reliable deployment.
Leads through technical vision, mentoring, cross-functional alignment, and architecture decision-making rather than formal executive org ownership.
experience_safe: Transformed a fragile legacy ad-tech platform into event-driven TypeScript microservices on AWS, reducing incidents by 75% and sustaining 3 years of zero downtime while continuing feature delivery.
Architected observability, monitoring, and incident-response capabilities, including an OpenSearch pipeline processing billions of events daily and proactive alerting workflows that reduced MTTR.

Led Commander-4 platform engineering for an enterprise AI workflow platform serving 2,000 users with 42 plugins, including hybrid retrieval, structured outputs, guardrails, access control, and semantic caching.
Architected a 0→1 event-sourced CRM platform using CQRS and EventStore, launching to production with 25 tenants and building growth runway for 10x scale.
Introduced DDD and bounded-context standards that cut onboarding from 6 months to 3 months, improved team velocity by 25%, and reduced requirement misalignment by 40%.
leadership_safe: Mentored 10+ engineers on architecture and cloud patterns and supported promotion of 3 engineers into lead roles.
Led technical hiring activities including selection criteria, interview strategy, and technical assessment design.
Facilitated stakeholder-facing architecture work through technical vision, event storming, subject-matter advisory, and transparent risk communication.
unsafe_or_too_weak: Head of AI, VP AI, Chief AI Officer, or Director of AI as a literal title claim.
Company-wide AI strategy ownership, enterprise-wide AI governance ownership, or department-level AI org leadership.
Budget/P&L accountability, large-scale org building, or formal performance management ownership at executive scope.
Broad enterprise multi-cloud leadership as a headline claim.
AI compliance-program, audit, or regulatory ownership beyond concrete controls and product-specific GDPR/TCF implementation.
citations: Blueprint: title inflation risks and management leadership weakness; budget/P&L 0/35 (0.0%).

well_represented_now: The current representation already surfaces flagship Seven.One modernization, architectural runway, observability, and DDD evidence tied to quantifiable outcomes.
The current project layer does show real AI platform delivery through Commander-4 and Lantern, including retrieval, routing, caching, and evaluation themes.
The existing summary anchors the profile in architecture and delivery, which is directionally correct for this category.
underrepresented_now: Architect-family title positioning is weaker than the underlying evidence base because the current headline is generic and the stronger architect-title variants remain upstream.
Stakeholder-facing advisory, business translation, and subject-matter-expert signals are not surfaced with enough prominence for this category.
Governance, guardrails, structured outputs, evaluation, and safe-production AI controls are present in curated project evidence but not elevated in the current summary/headline.
Player-coach leadership is present, but the representation does not cleanly distinguish it from management-heavy leadership language.
Transferable architecture depth from Samdock and earlier distributed-systems roles is underweighted relative to tool-centric AI skills.
overstated_risk_now: 'Engineering Leader' in the current headline can read more management-heavy than the actual evidence warrants for this category.
The skills-priority layer leans heavily into RAG and tooling names, which risks obscuring the stronger architecture, reliability, and governance signals.
Because the representation is proxy-derived, the current summary may overfit one flagship modernization story and understate the candidate's broader architecture portfolio.
representation_priorities: Reframe the headline toward architect-first AI/platform leadership using current safe titles and architecture language.
Rewrite the summary so it balances modernization, operational readiness, stakeholder translation, and production AI governance rather than relying on a single legacy-transformation paragraph.
Reorder project emphasis to foreground platform scope, guardrails, evaluation, reliability, and operational readiness before tool inventories.
Bring forward one stakeholder-facing architecture bullet and one AI governance/control bullet into the top role/project surfaces.
Use Samdock 0→1 architecture evidence as a secondary proof block to broaden the candidate beyond one employer and show transferable platform design depth.

curation_priorities_top.action: Promote the Seven.One architect-family title variants into reusable metadata, including 'Lead Software Engineer / Technical Architect' alongside the current Technical Lead framing.
curation_priorities_top.why_now: This is the highest-leverage curation action because the category is title-sensitive in an architect-first way, and the stronger architect framing already exists upstream with high-confidence provenance.
curation_priorities_top.expected_category_impact: Improves headline safety, ATS alignment, and category fit without inventing executive scope.
curation_priorities_top.citation: High-confidence upstream title evidence is available at docs/archive/knowledge-base.md:7, :98, :268, :360.

curation_priorities_top.action: Promote stakeholder-translation and architecture-advisory evidence from Seven.One into summary-ready metadata, especially SME collaboration, business-metric realization, and risk communication.
curation_priorities_top.why_now: Stakeholder translation is a category table-stakes signal, and the current representation under-surfaces it despite existing curated support.
curation_priorities_top.expected_category_impact: Strengthens architect/advisory positioning and reduces dependence on purely technical modernization framing.
curation_priorities_top.citation: Underlying curated evidence exists at data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:123, :250, :266; tracker ref docs/current/achievement-review-tracker.yaml:49 is prioritization only.

curation_priorities_top.action: Promote the AI governance and safe-production mechanisms from Commander-4/Lantern into top-level reusable summary/project metadata.
curation_priorities_top.why_now: These are category differentiators already supported by curated evidence, but they are not yet carrying enough weight in the master-CV representation.
curation_priorities_top.expected_category_impact: Raises differentiation on governance, guardrails, evaluation readiness, and production AI maturity.
curation_priorities_top.citation: Curated governance/control evidence is already present at data/master-cv/projects/commander4.md:8-9, data/master-cv/projects/lantern.md:9, and data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:332, :354.

curation_priorities_top.action: Broaden the curated architecture bench by surfacing Samdock's 0→1 event-sourced platform, event storming, and scalability architecture more explicitly in reusable metadata.
curation_priorities_top.why_now: The current fit depends heavily on Seven.One; Samdock offers credible secondary proof for transferable architecture leadership and stakeholder facilitation.
curation_priorities_top.expected_category_impact: Improves depth, reduces single-employer concentration, and reinforces platform-design credibility for architect-led AI leadership roles.
curation_priorities_top.citation: Supported by curated Samdock evidence at data/master-cv/roles/02_samdock_daypaio.md:27, :44, :142 and upstream role framing at docs/archive/knowledge-base.md:734.
